topic: A chef preparing Hand-Pulled Lanzhou Ramen (Lamian).
raw_generation:
{
  "script": "INT. KITCHEN - DAY
The camera glides across a bright kitchen. A MASTER CHEF stands at a wooden counter.
CHEF: Welcome to my kitchen. Today we make hand-pulled Lanzhou lamian, a traditional craft of dough and broth.

Dough prep
The ingredients are set on the counter: high-gluten flour, salt, and cold water.
[Action] 500 g flour weighed, 5 g salt added, 250 ml cold water poured in.
[Action] The dough is kneaded until smooth, wrapped, and rested for 25 minutes.

Dough conditioning
The resting period lets gluten develop and hydration even out.
[Action] After resting, the dough is gently kneaded again and formed into a long rope.
[Action] The bench is dusted with a light layer of flour to prevent sticking.

Hand-pulling routine
On a floured surface, the dough rope is folded and pulled, slapped, and folded again to create many strands.
The chef demonstrates with a calm rhythm: stretch, slap, twist, and loop until the strands are thin and uniform.

Broth preparation
A separate pot is started with beef bones, onions, garlic, and ginger. Aromatics are added with star anise and bay leaves for depth.
The stock simmers slowly, its aroma filling the kitchen as it clears and concentrates.

Noodle cooking and assembly
Fresh lamian noodles are boiled briefly until just tender, then drained.
In a warm bowl, noodles are laid out, topped with thin slices of beef, and finished with chopped green onion and cilantro.
A drizzle of chili oil and a spoon of sesame paste complete the bowl.

Final tasting
The chef tastes the bowl and nods with approval.
CHEF: The balance of clear beef broth, chewy hand-pulled noodles, and bright toppings is what makes this lamian special.

OUTRO
The camera pulls back to reveal a tray of finished bowls ready to serve.
",
  "narration": "Lanzhou lamian, meaning hand-pulled noodles from Lanzhou in Gansu Province, is one of China’s great noodle traditions. The magic begins with a simple dough: flour, water, a pinch of salt, mixed until smooth and left to rest so gluten relaxes.

In the hands of a skilled puller, the dough becomes a symphony of rhythm. The chef folds, pulls, slaps, and twists, turning a single rope into hundreds of delicate strands. Each pull requires control, flour on the surface, and a calm breath.

While the noodles rest in their delicate yarns, a stock pot begins its long simmer. Beef bones, onions, garlic, and ginger release their essence into a clear, deeply flavored broth. Hours of gentle simmering coax collagen, minerals, and aroma into every drop.

When the noodles are ready, they are boiled just briefly so they stay chewy and bright. A bowl is warmed, noodles settled inside, beef slices fanned on top, and the broth ladled over. Finishing touches—green onion, cilantro, a bright burst of chili oil, and a touch of sesame paste—complete the bowl.

The result is a bowl that looks simple but tastes expansive: a clean broth, a tangle of delicate noodles, and toppings that brighten each bite. This is lamian as a culinary art, a dance of dough, heat, and patience."
}

parsed_generation.script:
INT. KITCHEN - DAY
The camera glides across a bright kitchen. A MASTER CHEF stands at a wooden counter.
CHEF: Welcome to my kitchen. Today we make hand-pulled Lanzhou lamian, a traditional craft of dough and broth.

Dough prep
The ingredients are set on the counter: high-gluten flour, salt, and cold water.
[Action] 500 g flour weighed, 5 g salt added, 250 ml cold water poured in.
[Action] The dough is kneaded until smooth, wrapped, and rested for 25 minutes.

Dough conditioning
The resting period lets gluten develop and hydration even out.
[Action] After resting, the dough is gently kneaded again and formed into a long rope.
[Action] The bench is dusted with a light layer of flour to prevent sticking.

Hand-pulling routine
On a floured surface, the dough rope is folded and pulled, slapped, and folded again to create many strands.
The chef demonstrates with a calm rhythm: stretch, slap, twist, and loop until the strands are thin and uniform.

Broth preparation
A separate pot is started with beef bones, onions, garlic, and ginger. Aromatics are added with star anise and bay leaves for depth.
The stock simmers slowly, its aroma filling the kitchen as it clears and concentrates.

Noodle cooking and assembly
Fresh lamian noodles are boiled briefly until just tender, then drained.
In a warm bowl, noodles are laid out, topped with thin slices of beef, and finished with chopped green onion and cilantro.
A drizzle of chili oil and a spoon of sesame paste complete the bowl.

Final tasting
The chef tastes the bowl and nods with approval.
CHEF: The balance of clear beef broth, chewy hand-pulled noodles, and bright toppings is what makes this lamian special.

OUTRO
The camera pulls back to reveal a tray of finished bowls ready to serve.
parsed_generation.narration:
Lanzhou lamian, meaning hand-pulled noodles from Lanzhou in Gansu Province, is one of China’s great noodle traditions. The magic begins with a simple dough: flour, water, a pinch of salt, mixed until smooth and left to rest so gluten relaxes.

In the hands of a skilled puller, the dough becomes a symphony of rhythm. The chef folds, pulls, slaps, and twists, turning a single rope into hundreds of delicate strands. Each pull requires control, flour on the surface, and a calm breath.

While the noodles rest in their delicate yarns, a stock pot begins its long simmer. Beef bones, onions, garlic, and ginger release their essence into a clear, deeply flavored broth. Hours of gentle simmering coax collagen, minerals, and aroma into every drop.

When the noodles are ready, they are boiled just briefly so they stay chewy and bright. A bowl is warmed, noodles settled inside, beef slices fanned on top, and the broth ladled over. Finishing touches—green onion, cilantro, a bright burst of chili oil, and a touch of sesame paste—complete the bowl.

The result is a bowl that looks simple but tastes expansive: a clean broth, a tangle of delicate noodles, and toppings that brighten each bite. This is lamian as a culinary art, a dance of dough, heat, and patience.
